data.title: Rocket Lab: How The Market Turned Neutron Into A Sell Catalyst
data.description:
Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) stock has fallen 27.5% since my last report, and that was not completely unexpected, as the stock price had been priced to perfection while substantial risk on the Neutron development timeline and budget remained. In this report, I discuss the stock price development, the Neutron delay and cost overrun, assess the Q3 2025 results, and update my price target.

Rocket Lab Stock Price: What Goes Up Must Come Down

In my prior report, I ran fundamental analyses using various valuation methods and marked $66.89 as a realistic price target for Rocket Lab stock and a price at which point Rocket Lab would also become a sell in the sense that incrementally selling would be justified, and $72.63 would be a more upbeat price target, after which positions should be sold fully. While the stock price has fallen since my "Buy"-rated report, it should be noted that a 19% gain would be realized when taking the lower price target and a 29% gain when taking the higher price target following the price targets I highlighted.

So, while the stock price has declined 27.5% since my prior report, the price targets I set still would imply a gain. It shows how important it is to set fundamentally supported price targets for a stock that is gaining on a combination of long-term prospects and euphoria. Once euphoria fades for whatever reason, it is important to have some sense of what stock prices find support rooted in the fundamental and growth prospects.

Neutron First Rocket Launch Delayed To 2026 And More Costly

Neutron has often been cited as the catalyst for Rocket Lab’s stock price, and that is why many investors have been buying. I provided the following comment in my prior report:

While I am bullish on Rocket Lab, I believe that the reality is that Rocket Lab stock is not particularly cheap, as the stock has gained over 500% over the past year. I am not marking the stock a sell, but if you are willing to invest in Rocket Lab, I believe that incrementally adding to the position may be the best strategy, and as an investor, you should be aware that with the current stock prices, Rocket Lab cannot afford any hiccups in its Electron launch cadence or commercialization of the Neutron rocket. Any hiccup is likely to result in a substantial reduction to the stock price, which I believe would provide a more compelling entry point.

What happened with Rocket Lab’s stock price is that investors priced the company as if there was zero risk of delay in the development schedule and budget for Neutron, and what should have been a catalyst was already being priced in. Effectively, it meant there was no contingency for setbacks in the program, and the catalyst was actually no longer a positive catalyst. With no contingency in the stock price for setbacks, risk was inflated and created a catalyst in the sell direction. Rocket Lab pushed the first Neutron launch back from late 2025 to somewhere in 2026.

The reason for the delay is deeper qualification and acceptance testing to retire risk. The company aims to have the first mission be successful rather than taking an approach where they accept that the launch vehicle will blow up a few times before the first successful launch. I believe that is an approach that makes sense. It, however, comes with a price tag. Instead of $250-$300 million in costs for 2025, the company expects $360 million. I believe the recalibration of the timeline, cost estimate, and stock price fits any company that works on complex engineering products, and investors likely also needed the reminder that even with great prospects for a company such as Rocket Lab, risks always remain and the sky is not the limit, which holds quite literally for Rocket Lab’s business. There simply was too much euphoria in the stock price of Rocket Lab. I don’t view the delay as a sign that Rocket Lab did not deliver; the market just forgot that it is literally rocket science and priced in no downward scenario for the Neutron development, thereby essentially pre-pricing the Neutron catalyst.

Financial Results Continue To Improve

While the Neutron delay and budget expansion can be seen as a setback, the Q3 results showed continued growth. Revenues grew 48% year-on-year and 7% sequentially to $155.1 million. Launch sales almost doubled year-on-year, driven by higher launch activity, while Space Systems sales grew 36%. Sequentially, Launch services revenues declined 12%, driven by lower launch activity but partially offset by higher revenues per launch. Space Systems sales increased nearly 17%, contributing to margins expanding strongly from 36.9% to 41.9%. The company’s adjusted EBITDA was a $26.3 million loss compared to a $27.6 million loss in the prior quarter.

The company’s backlog grew 10% sequentially to $1.1 billion. So, despite higher revenues, we see that the backlog continues to grow, which puts a strong marker for even higher revenues. Around 57% of the backlog is expected to materialize over the next 12 months. It essentially shows that any backlog the company does add, adds to the growth prospects when compared to the Q3 2025 performance. Putting it differently, the backlog already has 13% growth in revenues locked in against the twelve-month-trailing revenues of $554.5 million.

Expectations Revised Downward For Rocket Lab

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Rocket Lab, the fundamentally supported price target for 2030 has declined substantially. Estimates for total EBITDA generation between 2025 and 2030 have come down by 5.2%, while free cash flow generation estimates have been revised down by 15.2%. The downward revisions are driven by the higher cost expected for the Neutron development and lower sales expectations by 2030, as well as a contraction of the peer group multiple. For Rocket Lab’s valuation, I use a multiple that is twice that of the aerospace and defense industry peer group, and that peer group multiple has declined, inducing additional downside to the stock price. As a result, the fundamentally supported stock price, while keeping in mind the commercialization path, is $48.88, providing 14% upside. Below a price of $35.07, I believe the stock would be a "Strong Buy."

While the expectations did come down, I do believe that the company still will exhibit strong growth prospects. The growth is just somewhat delayed. Cash flows are expected to ramp up almost linearly as all lines of business continue to grow. Furthermore, we note that the company is in a strong cash position. It is just the reality for Rocket Lab that most of the company’s value will be rendered beyond 2030.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Stock Now Reflects Reality Of Neutron Risk

I believe that the market had forgotten about the risk inherent to complex engineering projects and considered Rocket Lab stock lower risk than it actually was. I have been a bit less bullish as I also weighed the risk of Neutron schedule slips. Rocket Lab stock remains at trading levels substantially higher than those of peers, but an EV/EBITDA valuation does show there remains upside for the stock. The company is in a net cash position, so there is no need to issue additional shares to cover the additional Neutron costs. So, I am re-issuing my "Buy" rating, as the prior call from buy to sell has worked out and the stock price has reset.

data.title: Plug Power: Perpetual Capital Raises
data.description:
Following a big rally, Plug Power Inc. (PLUG) has completely round-tripped the recent rally after the company raised more capital. The green hydrogen solutions company has improved capital discipline, but the company is still far from producing profits, leading to the constant need to raise capital. My investment thesis is Bearish on the stock due to the ongoing operational issues.

Another Capital Raise

After the market close yesterday, Plug Power announced raising capital via a $375 million convertible debt offering due in 2033. The company is using the capital to repay ~$243 million of net proceeds for the outstanding principal amount of 15% secured debt.

The capital raise again reinforced fears of Plug Power constantly raising capital. The company just reported Q3 results, where another large loss was reported while revenues were remarkably flat with the prior year.

Plug Power has now burned nearly $500 million in cash in the YTD period. The company had reported cash used in operating activities of $387 million plus another $107 million in purchases of property and equipment.

The company raised another $370 million in early October by inducing the exercise of 185 million warrants at an exercise price of $2. Also, the deal involved issuing another 185 million warrants at an exercise price of $7.75, a price that seems unlikely but would lead to additional shareholder dilution to rise what could amount to $1.4 billion in additional capital.

Even more important, Plug Power appeared to dramatically shift the business plans with focusing on collaborating with data center providers and moving away from building hydrogen plans to provide fuel for customers. The company is dropping the pursuit of the large $1.66 billion DoE loan to build additional plants, instead pursuing plans to monetize electricity rights in New York and another location since while signing a competitively priced long-term hydrogen supply agreement, reducing the need for self-development of new plants.

Ironically, Plug Power has spent the last several years chasing hydrogen plants with current capacity of 40 tpd and jumping to 85 tpd after opening the massive Texas plant, though this location could be the unnamed location. The company was still losing about $30 million per quarter on fuel sales due to buying fuel from third-parties to fulfill customer contracts until new plants are built.

Smaller Losses Are Still Massive

Plug Power actually reported an adjusted gross loss for Q3'25 of only $36 million, a huge improvement over prior results. The company lost $86 million in the prior September quarter on the same revenue base.

Note, this is only the losses on generating revenues. Plug Power still has a large operating expense base of $127 million and reported a non-GAAP loss of $0.12.

With 1.159 billion average shares outstanding, the loss amounted to $139 million. Plug Power has a diluted share count much closer to 1.4 billion shares, especially after inducing the exercise of the 185 million warrants.

Even with better financial discipline, Plug Power hasn't come close to actually producing positive gross margins to offset operating expenses. Plug Power would need upwards of $425 million in quarterly revenues with a 30% gross margin to breakeven at the current spending level, which is a huge jump in both revenues and gross margins.

The company named Jose Luis Crespo as the new CEO effective in March with the filing of the annual 10-K. While the performance of Plug Power over the last few years warrants a change in the corporate leadership, new executives with new plans can disrupt the business before tangible benefits are seen by the investor base. The plans of the new CEO won't even come into focus until when the company reports the Q1 '26 results next May.

Plug Power has the goal to be EBITDAS positive in 2026 with a plan for operating income positive when exiting 2027. The company is still far away from reaching these goals in order to make Plug Power an interesting investment.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the recent capital raises reduce the need for immediate capital raises until the company has the potential to report improved financial results. Investors should avoid the stock until the new CEO can cut the current loses and show a path to consistent revenue growth, though the past history of Plug Power isn't very supportive of this outcome with green hydrogen sales constantly missing big forecasts.

data.title: WTI Oil Collapses As Ukraine-Russia War Nears Resolution (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Some reports from Axios have emitted the possibility of the Russia-Ukraine war going towards a US-led year-end resolution.

Some of this could have been anticipated – Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced yesterday that paths to direct discussion with Russia could reopen and that Ukraine is “preparing to reinvigorate negotiations”.

So the overnight report could just be one of diplomatic progress, in a US attempt to provide a plan similar Peace Plan as the one that was agreed in Gaza. The rest will be to see if both sides actually commit to the discussions.

The direct effect was some imminent selling in Oil (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) and related products, with Black Gold down $1.80 from its highs, or very close to 3%.

An argument could be that the war continuing was also a bearish catalyst, with Russia flooding Markets to sponsor its attacks on Ukraine.

So if the war ends, could supply drastically reduce? Would it necessarily lead to a rebound?

This could be an opportunity to fade the news but will be contingent on many other factors, the most important being positioning and how prices actually move at the news.

So let's take a look at that precisely in a multi-timeframe US Oil (WTI) technical analysis.

US Oil (WTI) Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

Oil still evolves within its Main Descending Channel formed throughout July and actually hangs around its middle, indicating somewhat of a balance in the price action.

Current trading remains within the May range between $59 and $60.5 that acts as long-timeframe support but also as imminent consolidation zone.

The RSI tilting towards the lower part gives the bears a slight technical advantage, but as long as the Daily Momentum remains above the 40 level, it's still largely considered neutral, implying further rangebound action.

Still remaining in a downwards channel, the action keeps a slight bearish but balanced tilt.

Let's dive deeper in the charts.

4H Chart and technical levels

Levels to place on your WTI charts:

Resistance Levels

  • Key September Resistance $65 to $66
  • Sep High timeframe Pivot $62 to $63
  • $60.90 Weekly highs

Support Levels

  • $59 to $60.50 2021 Support now Pivot (attempting to break)
  • $55 to $57 2025 Support
  • Oct 20 lows $56.38
  • $57.50 Hourly channel lows
  • Past Week lows $58.265

1H Chart

Sellers are making a move to push below the $59 support mentioned on the higher timeframe and could test the previous week lows at $58.56.

Keep an eye on the Hourly channel bounds which tend to act as strong support and resistance levels.

The 1H RSI is coming towards oversold, therefore continuation here would imply that sellers are keeping the short-term hand.

Watch what happens when and if prices reach the past week lows support ($58 to $58.50).

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: We Are Not Impressed By The MSC Income Fund Discount
data.description:
Many times in our articles, we have noted that business development company ("BDC") bonds have been among the most attractive investment opportunities and outperformed their common shares. Of course, we expected that this trend would not last forever, as everything is highly dependent on market sentiment. Therefore, we turn our attention again to the BDC sector, but this time to common shares, which are currently under serious pressure - trading at deep discounts and offering high yields. In this article, we will look at the company MSC Income Fund, Inc. (MSIF), managed by Main Street Capital (MAIN), and the opportunities presented by its common stock.

Basic Company Information

  • Total Assets 3rd Quarter: close to $1.29 billion.
  • Total Debt: around $529 million.
  • Total Equity: close to $734 million.
  • Currently Market Capitalization: approximately $622.9 million.
  • Portfolio:

The biggest part of the MSIF's portfolio is 9% in the "Electrical Equipment" industry and 8% in the "Commercial Services & Supplies."

  • Leverage and expenses

The total leverage ratio is around 41.65%, the total expense ratio is above 11%, and the gross asset non-leverage expense ratio is close to 3.3%. The 1-year z-score is negative 1.42.

Since October 30, 2020, the Fund has been externally managed by a wholly-owned subsidiary of Main Street Capital Corporation (Main Street), a publicly traded BDC (MAIN). Prior to October 30, 2020, Main Street served as the Fund's sub-adviser.

MAIN has strategically positioned itself to benefit from management fees, having built a formidable reputation in the sector. While this recognition is well-earned, it would be unrealistic to expect MSIF to match MAIN's performance in terms of NAV return.

Historical Performance

To examine the company, we have chosen several basic metrics that are the most important for us to determine the risk profile and the return capability in the future.

  • Assets Yield stripped of non-leverage expenses and realized and unrealized profits through the years. This gives us the yield after management fees and management performance if the entity were not to be leveraged:

On average, MSIF was earning around 6.51% on its assets adjusted for management fees and losses on the portfolio.

  • Non-leverage Expenses

Non-leverage expenses average 2.63% of total assets:

  • ROE

This is the key indicator that reflects the return on capital and its volatility. With almost 7 years of data, we can make a fairly reliable forecast for the future. The average ROE of MSIF has been around 4.21%.

  • Losses as a percentage of total income.

MSIF loses, on average, about 7.85% of its investing income:

Projections and Determining a Fair Value Discount

To simplify the model, we use several assumptions, as future yields and the yield curve are uncertain. We will assume an average SOFR for the next 5 years and adjust the asset return accordingly. Leverage costs are calculated based on historical spreads and debt structure. Operating expenses are around 2.6%. For portfolio losses, we use a historical average of 7.9% of total income.

Below, we can see what the expected ROE is based on how poorly we expect the portfolio to perform.

MSIF is currently trading at a price of $13.13, with a discount of 15.5% at its lows, with an average discount for the last year of 0.57%.

At a 15.5% discount, the projected NII yield is close to 8.63%, and the expected adjusted return is close to 8.6%.

For comparison, if we take the highest-yielding OTC bond on MAIN and leverage it against the corresponding swap rate, we see that its expected ROE is about 7.7%.

Credibility

MSIF does not have a credit rating, so we will estimate one using Moody’s framework:

  • Profitability: Here we get an ROA of about 2.3%, which corresponds to a Baa1 rating.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio): The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. MSIF has an ACR of 240%, which gives it a 60% buffer – one of the best in the sector – and a rating of Aaa.
  • Asset Quality: Non-accrual loans are 1.4% of the portfolio – equivalent to a Baa2 rating. -75% of assets are senior secured, which again carries a Baa2 rating.
  • Debt Structure: The debt distribution is weaker than that of ARCC, resulting in a low score on this criterion: Ba3. The secured debt is 42% of the assets, which equates to a Ba3 rating.
  • Final Rating: The weighted average credit score corresponds to A2, while the adjusted credit score corresponds to A3.

Conclusion

At the time of writing, MSIF is trading at a discount, close to its lows, to net asset value and offers an expected return even under more conservative loss scenarios. While sector bonds remain the safer alternative and the low valuations of BDCs create the opportunity for higher returns for the risk taken, which one will you prefer?

data.title: Nebius Group: Buy The Dip In This Quiet Giant In AI Hyperscaling
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Nebius Group N.V. ( NBIS), along with fellow "neocloud hyperscaler" CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), has clearly been an outlier this year. To back that up, Nebius stock alone has skyrocketed 211% year-to-date, while CoreWeave is up almost 90%. Both businesses have risen in the ranks, sealing a laundry list of deals with the biggest in tech.

Front and center for Nebius is its massive $17.4 billion contract to supply tech giant Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) with GPUs, as well as the more recent $3 billion AI-cloud deal with Meta Platforms, Inc. (META). Additionally, the company’s deep partnership with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) gives access to its cutting-edge GPUs, pushing ahead of its peers.

However, with all its promise, there is the AI bubble chatter for investors to contend with, but I feel there’s still a tremendous runway for Nebius, which hasn’t been priced in yet.

Hence, the recent dip in value (the 24% drop in the past month alone) is an excellent time to load up for more gains ahead. Naturally, there are several risks for investors to consider, yet its scale, mammoth deals, and pricing multiples are expected to contract sharply; I feel Nebius still remains a ‘buy’.

The Meta Deal Underscores Nebius' Long-Term Strategy

The massive $3 billion Meta deal is clearly headline-worthy, but what really stands out for me is how it effectively reshapes the company’s growth profile.

Picture this: at full run-rate, the deal spread across five years could yield nearly $600 million in annualized sales, blows past Nebius’s entire 2025 sales guidance at the midpoint ($525 million).

Putting it plainly, one contract from a second hyperscaler might actually “replace” the current business once it’s fully ramped up. It's a testament to how quickly the business has been scaling, underscoring growth through proper planning and reliable partnerships. Nebuis’s Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Arkady Volozh, bluntly said:

“Every time we brought capacity online, we sold all of it.”

In other words, the demand is as real as it gets, and Nebius is converting its capacity into sales immediately. Moreover, it also sheds light on the scarcity of AI infrastructure, which gives Nebius a massive edge, as it holds supply at the time when it matters most.

Volozh further claimed that the contract was limited by capacity, not demand:“If we had more, we could have sold more.”

That said, though, GPUs, power, and cooling still remain bottlenecks.

However, kicking things off with such a huge deal helps build trust while demonstrating that it can efficiently deliver.

The Meta deal is more than a marquee win as the potent partnership helps Nebius de-risk its lofty 2026 growth plans, with $7 billion to $9 billion in annual recurring revenue (ARR) already guided.

With the company’s hyperscaler deals backing the lion’s share of 2026-27 sales, Nebius is well-positioned for long-term expansion, even as it looks to pursue additional opportunities with startups and enterprises.

Volozh describes the Meta deal as follows:

This deal comes on top of the Microsoft deal… we expect to sign more large long-term deals, and we are delivering that promise.

Meta and Microsoft could have opted for AWS or Google, but instead they went with a relatively obscure Nebius. This shows confidence in the company's ability to process large-scale AI infrastructure, as we move into the latter half of the boom.

Q3 Performance Validates Capacity-Driven Growth

Nebius’s Q3 showing was superb, and it shows that its potent strategy continues to pay dividends.

The company’s sales surged 355% YOY and 39% sequentially to $146 million.

The lofty top-line showing was anchored by its core infrastructure expansion, which grew at an eye-popping 400% YOY and 40% sequentially, along with its adjusted EBITDA of around 19%. This indicates that efficiency levels are improving in tandem with the scale-up of its operations. Although other segments are in the red, the situation is likely to shift quickly through next year as new capacity is monetized.

Annualized, the core business reaches $551 million, with a total ARR forecast of$900 million to $1.1 billion by 2025. Interesting, growth seems to be constrained by capacity, and not demand.

Whatever units are brought online are sold.

Power, rack space, and cooling were efficiently managed to ensure high utilization. Also, contracted power targets went from 1 GW to 2.5 GW in 2026 to 800 MW - 1 GW by the end of this year. On top of that, more than 50% of the $7 billion to $9 billion ARR target has been booked.

The Q3 pipeline also rose sharply, jumping sequentially by 70% to $4 billion, but the deployment remains cap-bound. This gap provides Nebius with pricing power and leverage.

Capex shot up from $2 billion to $5 billion, which is understandable given the steep ramp-up with pre-committed deals offering cash, financing growth, and a higher-margin outlook.

Overall, Nebius is scaling quickly and efficiently, holding a structural advantage, and gaining strong financial momentum simultaneously..

Valuation and Stock Pullback

Let's be fair, Nebius and many of the popular AI stocks at this point are clearly trading at a premium valuation.

Below is the snapshot of Nebius' price chart. NBIS stock has surged over 350% in the past year, which, for obvious reasons, was driven by a growing enthusiasm for AI compute and front-loading around the big deals. That rally effectively “pre-paid” future growth into the stock price.

However, the stock has pulled back of late, but I feel it's a logical reaction to excessive CapEx, the fresh equity, and rising short-term losses.

The daily stock chart above, though, suggests the broader trend is still intact. We can gauge that the stock price has dropped toward the 100-day EMA (the green line), which is basically acting like the long-term “average path” of the stock with the line sloping upward (the trend’s intact!).

Additionally, the candles are tagging the lower Bollinger Band after spending the majority of September–October riding the upper band.

That combo of a powerful uptrend and then a retreat to the lower band is usually a typical correction after a big run, not the end of the move. On top of that, the RSI (the lower panel) has dropped from overbought levels above 70 to the 30–40 range, which in many ways debunks the whole bubble chatter.

Moving ahead, a glance at the consensus estimates on both lines, along with the relevant pricing metrics, throws greater weight behind the bull case.

Clearly, with the current sales estimates, the implied forward PS ratios stand at a lofty 40.3 times. However, those multiples are expected to compress over time to under 2x by 2028.

It is important to understand, though, that Nebius needs to continue killing it with its quarterlies for the market to continue assigning such generous multiples. So far, though, it’s doing everything right, and the pullback makes it even more attractive.

Risks To The Thesis

Nebius carries some risks that warrant attention.

To start with, Nebius is heavily reliant on Nvidia, and a potential supply snag or a sudden hike in GPU prices can postpone its build-out. Even the biggest in the tech space buckle under the pressures of GPU-related bottlenecks.

Secondly, the industry is highly competitive. Its peers, such as CoreWeave and cloud incumbents such as AWS and Google Cloud, are all after the big-ticket AI customers. This is a drag on prices and ultimately its margins.

Its high valuation is another risk. Nebius is operating at a negative P/E and is unlikely to break even before it achieves scale. It’s sensing the critical importance of investing in AI infrastructure and being involved with a large number of customers in supporting long-term growth.

However, it is deeply in the red as far as profits are concerned, which will raise concerns among near-term profit investors, especially with the AI bubble fears in play.

Moreover, Nebius has raised a large amount of capital to fund its growth. Indeed, it’s a double-edged sword. It supports growth but also creates dilution risk for existing shareholders. The company also plans an ATM share program that could sell up to 25 million shares.

Takeaway On Nebius Stock

In a nutshell, I consider Nebius to be a rising star in the AI/ cloud space, with its flagship contracts holding it firmly in place.

Its hyperscaler contracts and outsized Q3 performance highlight that its services with GPU-based infrastructure are in robust demand.

Yes, risks are present, especially about the supply of GPUs, capacity, cash burn, and valuation.

That said, its ambitious scale-up, recent deal momentum, as well as evident execution roadmap to achieve its targets can’t be denied. Hence, it’s a constructive long-term play and therefore a buy right now.

data.title: Kornit Digital: Upgrading On Business Model Transition And More - Buy
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Kornit Digital Ltd., or "Kornit" (KRNT), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Earlier this month, leading digital textile printing solutions provider Kornit Digital reported better-than-expected third quarter 2025 results, with both revenues and profitability coming in above the mid-point of the guidance ranges provided in the Q2 2025 press release:

While the company again lost money from operations, Kornit's large cash and liquid investment balances continued to generate material interest income. As a result, the company recorded $4.2 million of non-GAAP net income:

While gross margins were impacted by a combination of inventory adjustments, tariff costs and lower service contributions, persistent cost reduction initiatives resulted in non-GAAP operating expenses declining to new multi-year lows. Without unfavorable currency movements, operating expenses would have been even lower.

Kornit generated a small amount of free cash flow and ended the quarter with $489.8 million in cash and liquid investments or approximately $10.85 per common share. The company continues to have no debt.

On the conference call, management highlighted progress in Kornit's new All-Inclusive Click ("AIC") lease offering:

AIC is strengthening Kornit's leadership in digital production, serving as a clear differentiator that attracts new customers and generates strong momentum across the industry.

At the end of the third quarter, annual recurring revenue from AIC reached $21.5 million, up $2.6 million sequentially.

Several deals that shifted into early Q4 have since closed, bringing ARR to $23.1 million today, and we expect further expansion by year-end as the program continues to scale.

Going into 2026, Kornit expects to emphasize its AIC lease offering even further. While the new focus will result in lower sales growth and cash flow, the company's recurring revenue base is expected to increase quite meaningfully.

With lease offering contributions becoming more significant, Kornit will start to break out AIC revenues separately next year.

For the seasonally strong fourth quarter, the company expects sequential growth in both sales and profitability:

However, even at the high end of the provided ranges, Kornit's top- and bottom line would decline on a year-over-year basis.

On the conference call, management also provided an early outlook for 2026:

As we look ahead into 2026, we expect modest top line growth in the low-single digits as we continue to deliberately transition more customers to AIC while driving strong growth in annual recurring revenue.

At the same time, we expect continued EBITDA expansion driven by higher utilization, scaling recurring revenues and disciplined cost management. This evolution position Kornit for sustainable, profitable growth and long-term value creation.

On Monday, the company announced the appointment of a new CFO as well as a new $100 million share repurchase authorization

This authorization reflects the Company's strong balance sheet and continued confidence in the Company’s strategy, execution, and long-term growth trajectory. It builds on the $165 million in share repurchases executed since 2023 and underscores Kornit’s commitment to disciplined capital allocation and enhancing value for its shareholders.

For my part, I applaud the company's efforts to increase recurring revenues despite resulting short-term pressures on revenues and cash flows.

In addition, the new repurchase program should support the share price going forward.

However, with the business model transition still in early innings, it might take some time before progress becomes visible in Kornit's financial results.

With 2026 approaching quickly, I am now basing my valuation on 15x estimated 2027 EV/EBITDA (in line with the U.S. machinery industry):

Given 35% upside from current levels, I am upgrading Kornit Digital's shares from Hold to Buy with a price target of $17.50.

Please note that the company is dirt cheap from an EV/Sales perspective. At this point, the business is trading at just 0.5x estimated 2026 sales as compared to 2.9x for the broader industry.

Risk Factors

As evidenced in recent quarters, macroeconomic uncertainties could negatively impact customer demand. In addition, poor execution of the company's business model transition would likely result in shares dropping to new multi-year lows.

Bottom Line

While Kornit Digital reported somewhat better-than-expected third quarter results, the business remains impacted by macroeconomic uncertainties and the new focus on lease offerings which is putting some short-term pressure on revenues and cash flows.

For my part, I consider the company's efforts to increase recurring revenues the right move. In addition, the new $100 million share repurchase program should support the share price going forward.

Given 35% upside from current levels, I am upgrading Kornit Digital's shares from Hold to Buy with a price target of $17.50.

However, with the business model transition still in early innings, it might take some time until market participants warm up to the story.

data.title: Defense Stocks And ETFs Such As SHLD Are Declining Today: Why?
data.description:
Every time it looks as if the war in Ukraine will come to an end, these stocks plunge, especially the European firms, where some politicians would rather spend on other areas than on the defense of their nations.

This is not the first time a U.S.-led push to end the conflict has knocked these firms down. However, even if every single conflict worldwide were to end today, another dozen would just as likely arise tomorrow. Humans are a troublesome species.

I do not see a reduced future demand for military equipment. Quite the contrary. I see a rising demand for more technologically advanced weaponry that costs far more than yesterday’s tanks, ships, and aircraft. No one hates war more than a veteran. But we are also realists. If there must be trouble, let us have the equipment and weaponry to defend ourselves.

Even if fewer total weapons are sold, the dollar value of the ones that are produced will keep the defense industry profitable. Items that do not have a huge ticket, like today’s Gen 5 and tomorrow’s Gen 6 aircraft, will instead have such a sheer volume of production that it will lead to greater profitability. Think hundreds of thousands of drones, each by itself providing only a small profit – but recognize that these are more expendable items that must be replaced regularly.

What to do now?

If you do not own shares of companies in the business of defending one’s homeland, i.e., the defense industry, this would be a good time to nibble. You can always add more if this week or this month, today's defense industry investors believe that the world is going to unilaterally disarm and start singing Kumbaya together.

If you buy, remember that defense stocks and ETFs will always be highly sensitive to geopolitical events.

If you do own shares, you likely have nice profits, having held for a longer period of time. I personally have no problem taking some money off the table and placing “Buy” orders at various points below today’s prices. I am doing so today in some of my own accounts as well as in our Growth & Value Portfolio at The Investor’s Edge® here on Seeking Alpha.

What to buy if that is your decision?

I prefer buying strategically rather than tactically. I am not looking for “the one best drone company” or the “one company that is likely to get the next massive aircraft contract.” That is a Fool’s Errand.

These contracts, whether in the U.S., the U.K., France, Italy, India, or anywhere else, are subject to far more decision factors than most investors are aware of. There are financial, favoritism, prior history, great public relations, intense lobbying, political, geopolitical, and a score of other considerations.

In the USA, for instance, it depends as much on which Senator or Representative’s home state can get the business as it does on who can deliver the best product at the best price. In too many Republican administrations, Red states tend to get more consideration; in Democratic administrations, Blue states lobby for the same considerations. As Casey Stengel used to say, “You could look it up.”

That is why I choose a strategic approach. I own three ETFs that give me coverage of the primary defense firms in the Free World.

I predict that not-yet-public companies like Anduril and General Atomics, with expertise in the latest battlefield and sea- and airspace technologies, will take market share from many of their bigger, older, less responsive to innovation defense competitors. Owning ETFs with deep pockets and a charter to buy within the defense industry provides me with a greater likelihood of seeing these companies in my portfolio when they IPO (or before.)

What is the best way to stay on the cutting edge of defense spending as a result of defense innovation? I have selected three ETFs for this purpose for myself, our The Investor’s Edge ® subscribers, and my clients. They are:

If you are considering buying just one defense ETF, I think the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) is a good first choice. As the name implies, it seeks defense technology winners. Warfare is undergoing a major revolution. Drone warfare in Ukraine has changed everything, as it has dominated the battle space.

Instead of having a “front line” that is geographically close to both protagonists, there is now a “no man’s land." For many miles, neither side dares attempt to penetrate beyond their own counter-drone drones for (the very rational) fear of being obliterated by drones.

SHLD seeks out the innovative companies that are changing the face of modern warfare. I think that, over time, they will likely hold even more such companies – even if they have to sell some shares of today’s biggest contractors.

  • SHLD: The Pause That Refreshes.

The Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF (EUAD) is the direct beneficiary of NATO’s decision by all but two of its members to begin spending more on Europe’s defense, ultimately culminating in spending 3.5% of their nation’s GDP on defense and another 1.5% on dual-use infrastructure for defense. (Examples of dual-use might include new airports capable of also sustaining jet fighters with reinforcement and extended runway length.)

European defense spending is already starting to surge and EUAD has the biggest companies that will be the first to receive the biggest contracts.

European nations have received a wake-up call. They are no longer standing around waiting for the U.S. to pop open the defense umbrella for them every time they fear an aggressive neighbor like Russia. Indeed, Russia is testing every day with aircraft or drone incursions against Finland, Norway, Poland, Romania, Estonia, Denmark, and other NATO nations.

  • EUAD ETF: The 'Other' Way To Profit As Rearmament Of Europe Begins.

That brings us to the WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund ETF (WDEF), a brand new (since July 17, 2025), still low-volume, little-traded-as-yet, defense ETF. (Use limit orders if buying!) I add this possibility for your consideration because it spreads a larger net than EUAD. Some of the lesser-known in the U.S., smaller firms in Europe are, I believe, destined to grow bigger and stronger.

Innovation is not the exclusive domain of the U.S., China, and Russia. I can easily make the case that the (Swedish) Gripen JAS39’s E variant is not only a superior aircraft in war-damaged terrain and rugged infrastructure than the U.S. F-16, but it may even be more appropriate for war zones like eastern Ukraine than the more expensive Lockheed F-35.

EUAD also owns Europe’s big commercial “aircraft + defense” companies. I like having a bit of redundancy by owning both ETFs. WDEF holds many more positions, with an emphasis on pure defense (as opposed to EUAD's ownership of Airbus and other commercial aircraft).

Among these other positions are other European aircraft manufacturers like Dassault, maker of the Rafale multi-role fighter jet, and Norway’s Kongsberg Gruppen, which manufactures advanced missile systems in Norway, Australia, and, currently under construction, a factory in Virginia, USA.

  • Defense ETFs: WDEF Is Now The Third Leg Of Our Defense Stool.

I hold all three of these ETFs and have benefited from the overlaps among them. You may choose one approach alone. There are other defense ETFs, of course. I am not a fan of the ETFs that are primarily yesterday's giants. That is not to say they can not adapt, but there will likely be more stumbles a la Boeing (BA) along the way. There is nothing "wrong" with PPA, ITA, and XAR. But for me, there is more that is "right" and more that is specifically targeted than the big ETFs. For me, my three choices are the real trifecta.

The world is not growing any safer. Tyrants and autocratic dictators are not in decline. The Free World must let them know it would be a fool’s errand to try to dominate our nations. A strong defense is what will prevent them from such machinations.

Good “defensive” investing,

I do not know your personal financial situation, so I offer my opinions above for your due diligence, not as advice to buy or sell specific securities.

data.title: TCPA: A 6.25% Junior Subordinated Notes IPO From TC Energy
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This is our next in line article, in which we turn our attention to a recent exchange-listed debt issue: TC Energy Corporation's (TRP) 6.25% Junior Subordinated Notes (TCPA) due 11/01/2085. As usual in this type of analysis, we'll run the new debt issue through our fixed-income IPO evaluation routine to determine if it can be a valuable addition to our portfolio. The examination will begin with a brief study of the issuing company to assess the extent to which we can feel safe holding its subordinated notes. After that, we'll conduct a relative evaluation of TCPA, comparing its metrics to peers with similar metrics.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information about the newly listed baby bond TCPA. Here, we will highlight only the most important metrics for the analysis that follows.

TCPA pays 6.25% annual interest in quarterly distributions of $0.3906 each. Interest on the junior subordinated notes accrues from October 9, 2025, starting distributions on February 1, 2026. This means the first interest payment will be irregular in size, amounting to approximately $0.486. The company's gross proceeds from the new security are $350 million for a total of 14 million notes issued with a liquidation value of $25 each. The new baby bond is rated BBB- by S&P, Baa3 by Moody's, and BBB- by Fitch at the date of its IPO. TCPA is a long-term junior debt obligation of TC Energy with a maturity date of 11/01/2085 and is callable on and after 11/01/2030.

As the issue is just listed on the exchange, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, TCPA is priced at $24.50 with a Yield to Maturity calculated by the XIRR function of 6.57%. The tables below show the beginning of the internal rate of return calculation and how the YTM of the security would change with a price variation around the current price:

In a chart visualization:

Using the same internal rate of return method, the Yield to Call is calculated to be 7.04%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

The Company basics

Founded in 1951 and headquartered in Alberta, Canada, TC Energy Corporation is focused on generating, storing, and delivering energy products to households and companies in North America. The business of TC Energy is divided into two major segments - Natural Gas Pipelines and Power and Energy Solutions. The natural gas pipeline network supplies local distribution companies and direct clients in Canaga, the US, and Mexico.

The Power and Energy Solutions has a capacity of approximately 4.65 GW of power generation by nuclear, natural gas, wind, and solar sources, with the intention for that number to be increased in the future:

Company Performance

This is how the general market has evaluated the common stock of the company, TRP, for the past 10 years:

We'll compare the total return performance for the past ten years of TC Energy to that of the general market, represented by the S&P 500, to the First Trust North American Energy Infrastructure Fund ETF (EMLP), as a representative for the oil and gas midstream, and to the Tortoise North American Pipeline Index ETF (TPYP), as a specific representative for the average North American gas pipeline company:

The comparison shows that in the total return metric, TRP outperforms by far the S&P 500, EMLP, and TPYP. TC Energy is showing more than twice the results of the sector-specific ETFs.

That being pointed out, the expected future performance of TC Energy is more on the cautious side, according to the majority of the SA Analysts and the Quant rating, giving the company the neutral "Hold" score. The Wall Street analysts are more optimistic regarding the TRP's future performance, with an average "Buy" rating.

The direct comparison of TC Energy to peers of similar size from the Oil and Gas Storage and Transportation industry shows again that TRP is the top performer of the set, managing in the past couple of months to surpass Cheniere Energy, Inc. (LNG):

Capital Structure

Below is TC Energy's capital structure as of the Third Quarter Filing of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

According to the information from the Balance sheets, as of the end of September 2025, TC Energy had $44.13 billion in total debt, and TCPA will increase that number by $350 million to a total of approximately $44.48 billion. Junior to the debt of the company is the preferred equity of approximately $1.8 billion, and at the lowest position in the capital structure is the total common equity of $17.94 billion, with an additional non-controlling interest of $7.27 billion. Of the total debt outstanding, approximately $33.7 billion is in senior debt issues, which in the debt structure have priority over subordinated debt, such as TCPA. Regarding the subordinated debentures, the equity coverage is less than we'd ideally like to see.

Dividends

TRP has an impressive dividend history of 35 consecutive years of uninterrupted distributions, the last 25 of them of dividend growth.

TRP's TTM dividend amounts to approximately $2.40, and with the last closing common share price of $55.23, the stock's distribution yield is 4.34%. This is what the dividend yield has looked like for the past 10 years:

The forward estimations for TRP's distribution and dividend yield look like this:

TC Energy has approximately 1.04 billion common shares outstanding. With a $2.42 projected forward annual distribution on each of them, we calculate approximately $2.52 billion in common shares' dividends annually. The company had annual TTM total interest expenses of approximately $2.3 billion, and the new issue will increase this number by $22 million. On an annual basis, the common shares' distribution is enough to cover all of the expected total interest expenses. This is a good indication for the potential creditors of the company.

Credit Rating

Fitch Ratings - New York - 03 Jul 2025: Fitch Ratings has affirmed TC Energy Corporation's (TRP) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'BBB+' and its preferred shares at 'BBB-'. Fitch has also affirmed TransCanada PipeLines Limited's (TCPL) Long-Term IDR at 'BBB+', senior unsecured debt at 'BBB+', junior subordinated debt at 'BBB-', Short-Term IDR at 'F2', and CP at 'F2'.

In addition, Fitch has affirmed TransCanada Trust's (Trust) junior subordinated rating at 'BBB-'. Trust's junior subordinated notes are guaranteed by TCPL on a subordinated basis.

....

The Rating Outlook for all four companies is Stable.

This is the beginning of the recent rating action commentary with which Fitch confirms the "BBB+" long-term issuer default rating with a Stable Outlook of TC Energy, and the "BBB-" credit score for the junior subordinated debt. The credit score of TRP has remained unchanged by Fitch Ratings since March 2023, when it was downgraded from 'A-' to the current 'BBB+'.

Peers comparison

The Oil and Gas Midstream industry does not have other listed debt issues, so I'll use the Utilities baby bonds, priced below par and with investment-grade credit quality, for the peers comparison.

The securities with their metrics:

The comparison shows that TCPA is priced at a slightly better YTM, compared with the recent long-term utilities debt issues XELLL and DTK, with the same 6.25% nominal yields. However, for me, the majority of the issues priced at a deep discount below the PAR value are more attractive as both shorter-term trading opportunities and long-term investment vehicles.

If you are expecting a recent downshift in the long end of the yield curve, the priced way below par baby bonds are offering better price-appreciation potential, compared to TCPA, DTK, and XELLL. On the other hand, for the long-horizon investors, again, the deeply discounted low nominal baby bonds have more appeal. They are less likely to be called in the future, compared to the 6.25% nominal ones priced just below PAR, so more suitable for a long-term investment holding. For the extra cautious investors, ELC, EAI, and EMP offer way safer exposure.

Use of Proceeds

We intend to use the net proceeds from the offering to fund the redemption price of TCE’s 10,000,000 outstanding Cumulative Redeemable First Preferred Shares, Series 11 (the “Series 11 Preferred Shares”), to reduce indebtedness of the Corporation and for general corporate purposes. Pending any such use of the net proceeds, we will invest the net proceeds in short-term marketable debt instruments.

Source: TCPA Prospectus

Special Optional Redemptions

Prior to November 1, 2030, upon or following the occurrence of a Tax Event, we may, at our option, on giving not more than 60 nor less than 10 days’ notice to the Noteholders, redeem all (but not less than all) of the Notes at a redemption price per U.S.$25 principal amount of such Notes equal to 100% of the principal amount thereof, together with accrued and unpaid interest to but excluding the date fixed for redemption

Prior to November 1, 2030, upon or following the occurrence of a Rating Event, we may, at our option, on giving not more than 60 nor less than 10 days’ notice to the Noteholders, redeem all (but not less than all) of the Notes at a redemption price per U.S.$25 principal amount of such Notes equal to 102% of the principal amount thereof, together with accrued and unpaid interest to but excluding the date fixed for redemption.

Source: TCPA Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face value outstanding of the new exchange-traded baby bond TCPA of $350 million makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

The company has a long history of solid performance, with no apparent sign of deterioration. The dividend coverage of the interest payments is quite good, but the capital structure is heavy in senior debt. TCPA's comparison to direct peers shows better alternatives in both short and long-term holding scenarios. At the moment, it presents no investment interest for me.

data.title: XLE: Unprecedented Decoupling From Oil Price Is A Warning Sign
data.description:
In my last article on the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) in January, I argued for a potential bounce due to the breakout in oil prices. This bounce proved very short-lived, and oil prices have continued to trend lower, yet the XLE has completely ignored this decline, rising a further 6% since then. This decoupling between oil prices and oil stocks has been unprecedented and has left the XLE extremely overvalued. The ETF is now highly likely to underperform the oil itself.

The XLE tracks the performance of the Energy Sector Select Index, which tracks S&P 500 components involved in the development or production of energy products. It is a highly concentrated ETF with Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) comprising over 40%.

A Major Breakdown Between Oil Stocks And Oil Price

The XLE has been on the market since 1999 and has typically moved in line with oil prices, as one would expect. Over the past two years, as US stocks in general have surged higher, energy stocks too have ignored the decline in oil prices. I wrote about this disconnect a year ago, and since then it has become even more extreme.

The scale of divergence between the XLE and oil prices is unprecedented, and this has started to show up in the ETF's valuations. As sales have fallen 20% from their 2023 peak and profit margins have contracted by 6 percentage points, the Energy Sector Select Index's PE ratio has risen to 18x from single digits two years ago.

This figure is likely to go higher still over the next 12 months as the impact of lower oil prices takes time to feed through into XLE sales. Current 12-month trailing sales for the XLE are a combined USD1.28trn, but based on their correlation with oil prices, this should fall to around USD1.05trn over the next years. Even if the sector can manage to maintain its current net profit margins of 7.5%, this would put the forward PE ratio at 22x.

Free Cash Flow Likely To Continue Underperforming Earnings

On a free cash flow basis, the Energy Sector Select Index's multiple is now above 20x as capital spending has risen sharply relative to reported depreciation and amortization charges. The oil majors have engaged in huge investment over the past two years, which has taken capital expenditure back to 2016 levels. To maintain oil production at current levels, oil majors will likely need to ramp up capex after decades of declines relative to sales, which will eat into shareholder returns.

Talking of shareholder returns, XLE components have already begun scaling back on their dividend payouts, which have fallen 13% since their 2023 peak. This has taken the ETF's dividend yield down to 3.2%, a far cry from the double-digit figures seen in the wake of the Covid crash.

Long Oil, Short Oil Stocks

Investors are likely to be better off holding oil versus oil stocks over the next few years as the latter faces additional risks from a broader stock price weakness (see here). While it is often the case that oil prices are more volatile to the downside during stock market crashes, there is potential for stocks in general to move much lower even without a deflationary episode, which would benefit oil prices on a relative basis. A stagflationary scenario where rising costs put upside pressure on oil but economic weakness compresses stock valuations looks increasingly likely over the coming years.

Summary

The ratio of oil stocks relative to oil prices has reached new extremes, and this leaves the XLE exposed to weak long-term returns and downside risks in the event of broader equity market weakness. Oil bulls are likely to be much better off in the commodity itself than in the XLE.

data.title: CrossAmerica Partners LP: A Deleveraging, High Yield Story
data.description:
Units of motor fuel distributor and service station owner-operator CrossAmerica Partners LP (CAPL) currently yield 10% as concerns grow as to whether its distribution policy is sustainable. Its coverage dipped briefly to 0.79x in H1 '25 as higher interest expense and weak consumer demand compelled it to sell assets to pay down debt and cover its $0.525 per unit quarterly disbursement. With a streak of 30 consecutive $0.525 quarterly distributions, the recent small insider buying by each the co-founder and vice-chairman merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

CrossAmerica Partners LP is an Allentown, Pennsylvania-based wholesale distributor of motor fuel to more than 1,600 gas stations across the U.S., having supplied 1.3 billion gallons in 2024, making it one of the ten largest independent distributors in the country. It also owns or leases ~960 service stations, of which ~600 are leased or sublet to different classes of trade. The partnership also generates revenue from its company-operated retail stores. With roots back to 1987, CrossAmerica was formed as Lehigh Gas in 1992 and went public in 2012, raising net proceeds of $125.7 million at $20 per unit. It entered the retail business in 2013 and changed to its current moniker when the general partner (Lehigh) sold its interest to CST Brands in 2014. Units of CAPL trade around $21.00 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $800 million.

Operating Segments

The partnership views the performance of its operations through two segments: Wholesale and Retail.

Wholesale entails the sale of gasoline to 639 independent dealers and 365 lessee dealers (as of June 30, 2025), the latter of which leases their service stations from Cross America. Its fuel is supplied by major integrated oil companies and refiners of which ~95% is branded. For this service, the partnership essentially receives a markup typically between $0.08 and $0.09 per gallon. This division also includes rent received from its lessee dealers. It was responsible for operating income of $37.2 million on total gross profit of $51.5 million in 1H25.

Retail consists of the sale of motor fuel and convenience merchandise at its company operated service stations, as well as retail sites operated by commission agents. In the latter arrangement, the commission agent is responsible for the convenience store and receives a commission (typically $0.08 to $0.10 per gallon) on any fuel sold. CrossAmerica retains title to the fuel inventory and also generates revenue through leasing or subletting real estate to the commission agents. The gross profit on a gallon of gas is approximately $0.35 to $0.40 before any commission or credit card fees are paid with the partnership generally benefitting from volatility in fuel prices, putting truth to the adage that service stations are slow to reflect declines in oil prices but quick to raise them in response to increases. Gross profit contributions are typically low 50% from fuel and low 40% from merchandise, with the balance from rent and other revenue.

Those percentages are somewhat a function of how many commission agent locations (vis a vie wholly owned and operated properties) are in operation at any one time. Retail generated H1 '25 operating income of $36.8 million on total gross profit of $139.3 million.

Given that management is constantly reallocating properties by class of trade (independent dealer, lessee dealer, commission agent, owner-operator) prior-year comparisons amongst its operating segments aren’t necessarily meaningful. Overall business is seasonal with the summer driving season representing peak demand, with the second half of the year typically stronger than the first half.

Putting it altogether, CrossAmerica generated H1 '25 Adj. EBITDA of $61.4 million on gross profit of $190.8 million versus Adj. EBITDA of $66.1 million on gross profit of $186.1 million in H1 '24.

Approach & Recent History

The partnership is continually purchasing and selling assets, having acquired over 1,000 sites and more than 700 wholesale fuel supply contracts for a consideration of over $1.5 billion since going public, with each property evaluated for its optimal class of trade. When net leverage is elevated, CrossAmerica will sell some non-strategic real estate to pay down debt, usually maintaining the exclusive rights to supply the location with petrol.

After leveraging its floating-rate credit facility to acquire 106 properties from 7-Eleven for $276.6 million (primarily in 2021), the partnership’s net leverage spiked to 5.1 at YE21. With rates at historic lows, CrossAmerica initially enjoyed exceptionally low interest expense, further enhanced by its entrance into several extremely favorable swap contracts (0.298% to 0.413%) on $300 million of its $700 million plus of total debt. However, when those elevated obligations butted up against a subsequent expanding rate environment, the partnership’s interest expense increased from $18.2 million in FY21 to $32.1 million in FY22 and with its advantageous swaps terminating in FY24, that line item increased to $52.3 million last year. It was $25.4 million in 1H25.

Along with a cash-strapped American consumer who has eschewed excess gasoline consumption and relative price stability in the energy complex, the spike in interest expense has been responsible for the severe contraction of CrossAmerica’s distribution (dividend) coverage ratio from 1.77x in FY22 to 1.08x in FY24. With that metric shrinking to 0.79x in H1 '25 (versus 0.95x in H1 '24) and to 1.00x on a TTM month basis (versus 1.32x in the prior year period), the partnership was compelled to unload assets, registering record divestitures of ~$64 million (60 properties) during Q2 '24 to reduce debt and ensure it had enough cash to cover its dividend. These divestures continued to a lesser degree in the third quarter.

Q2 '25 Financials

Those divestitures were brought to light as part of CrossAmerica’s 2Q25 financial report on August 6, 2025, when it posted Adj. EBITDA of $37.1 million and distributable cash flow of $22.4 million on gross profit of $101.0 million versus Adj. EBITDA of $42.6 million and distributable cash flow of $26.1 million on gross profit of $104.7 million in 2Q24, representing declines of 13%, 14%, and 4%, respectively. As stated previously, the biggest factor in the declines was the frugal American driver, with Retail segment volumes down 1% year-over-year to 114.7 million gallons and Wholesale same store volumes down 2% to 179.2 million gallons. Wholesale fuel margin fell 2% to $0.085 per gallon as prompt payment discounts to its suppliers were smaller due to lower crude oil and gasoline prices.

Q3 2025 Results:

CrossAmerica Partners posted its Q3 results on November 5th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 34 cents a share, 27 cents a share above the consensus. Net income to $13.6 million, from $10.7 million in the same period a year ago. Distributable cash flow rose slightly to $27.8 million from $27.1 million while Adjusted EBITDA fell to $41.3 million from $43.9 million in 3Q2024. Operating expenses dropped five percent to $57.5 million.

Revenues fell 10% on a year-over-year basis to $971.8 million, some $190 million above the consensus. Volume decreased to 141.8 million of retail fuel gallons distributed compared to 148.4 million gallons for the third quarter of 2024.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Besides stating that it was performing better than the industry average and that its pipeline of asset sales for the balance of 2025 was robust, the best aspect of the second quarter was that its record asset sales allowed management to reduce its debt by $51.0 million to $727.0 million. CrossAmerica sold 29 properties for $21.9 million in proceeds in Q3, which lowered debt further to just over $705 million. As of the end of the third quarter, its debt-covenant-defined leverage has been lowered 4.36 at YE24 to 3.56. The current effective interest rate on its debt is just over six percent.

For now, CrossAmerica intends to keep its quarterly distribution at $0.525 per share, a level it has maintained since 2018, representing a normally eyepopping yield of 10%. The distribution coverage ratio for Q3 was 1.39 times compared to 1.36 times for the same period a year ago.

Street coverage for the partnership is nonexistent, and with no forecast from management, an investment decision rests on the fulcrum of confidence in its ability to maintain its current $0.525 quarterly disbursement. This does seem more secure after Q3 as the company continues to reduce debt.

Founder and Chairman of the Board Joseph V. Topper Jr. as well as Vice Chairman John B. Reilly III seem to be telegraphing that the distribution will continue based on their respective (albeit token) 1,801 and 1,871 share purchases on October 10, 2025. Their combined ownership stakes equate to 18% of the partnership.

Verdict:

The last full year in which CrossAmerica’s distribution coverage ratio dipped below 1.00 was FY17 (0.97). The following year, it lowered its quarterly distribution per unit from $0.628 to its present level. Prior to that time however, its quarterly distributions were more variable, strictly a function of distributable cash flow.

Rising interest rates on debt, weakening consumer demand, and low energy complex price volatility are compelling the partnership to sell properties in order to manage interest expense and cover its distribution. The take here is that despite the token purchases by insiders, and a yield of 10% - the lack of any growth prospects makes me neutral on CAPL shares.
